description: Multi-jurisdiction prosecuted procurement fraud cases used for Multi-Jurisdiction Procurement Integrity Standard (MJPIS) threshold derivation. v0.1 seeded with US DOJ cases only; expanded to UK SFO, French PNF, and World Bank INT in later research phases.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Price Inflation
cases.case_name: United States v. Oracle Corporation
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2011-08-01
cases.notes: Failed to provide promised educational discounts, charged government 200-400% more than commercial customers. GSA schedule contract violation.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Price Inflation
cases.case_name: United States v. Boeing Company
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2006-05-31
cases.notes: Charged Air Force 300-600% markup on standard aircraft spare parts with known commercial prices. Largest defense contractor overcharge settlement at the time.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Labor Rate Fraud
cases.case_name: United States v. DynCorp International
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2005-03-15
cases.notes: Billed senior engineer rates for junior staff, 50-100% above market rates. State Department contract for overseas operations support. Empirical floor for markup-based detection (75% is the lowest documented markup in DOJ corpus).

cases.legal_basis: Anti-Kickback Act + Procurement Integrity Act
cases.case_name: United States v. United Technologies Corporation
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2015-07-02
cases.notes: Paid consulting fees to procurement official's firm, received sole-source contract awards. Quid pro quo pattern combined with price inflation. Defense systems contracts.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Product Defects + Price
cases.case_name: United States v. Lockheed Martin Corporation
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2012-12-20
cases.notes: Sold defective aircraft parts while charging premium prices. Quality control failures combined with price inflation. Aircraft components for DoD.

cases.legal_basis: Procurement Integrity Act
cases.case_name: United States v. Northrop Grumman Corporation
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2009-06-25
cases.notes: Hired procurement official immediately after contract award. Official had influenced decision while in government. Conflict of interest pattern combined with inflated pricing. Defense electronics.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Time and Price Fraud
cases.case_name: United States v. CACI International Inc.
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2010-11-17
cases.notes: Charged for unworked hours, inflated labor rates 240% above contract terms. Timesheet fraud combined with rate inflation. IT services contract with Department of Interior.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Product Defects
cases.case_name: United States v. Raytheon Company
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2014-04-30
cases.notes: Sold radar components that failed quality tests, charged premium prices. Product testing failures combined with inflated pricing. Defense electronics for DoD.

cases.legal_basis: False Claims Act - Labor Fraud
cases.case_name: United States v. BAE Systems plc
cases.source_document: DOJ Press Release, 2010-03-01
cases.notes: Charged unrelated labor costs to government contracts, inflated billable hours. Timecard manipulation and cost allocation fraud. Defense services contracts.

cases.legal_basis: Conspiracy to corrupt (Criminal Law Act 1977 s.1); False accounting (Theft Act 1968 s.17); Failure to prevent bribery (Bribery Act 2010 s.7)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Rolls-Royce plc
cases.source_document: SFO v Rolls-Royce plc Approved Judgment (Leveson P), 2017-01-17; indictment U20170036; additional source: https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/67c061c4b0bb6528ee866bc3/SFO-Rolls-Royce_DPA_Details_of_Compliance.pdf
cases.notes: Largest UK criminal corporate penalty at time of DPA. UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement approved 17 January 2017 by Sir Brian Leveson P, indictment U20170036. Total penalty GBP 497,252,645 (~USD 604.8M historical): GBP 258.17M disgorgement of profit + GBP 239.08M financial penalty + GBP 13M SFO costs. Part of USD 800M global settlement including parallel US DOJ proceeding (USD 169,917,710) and Brazilian MPF proceeding (USD 25,579,170). 25+ years of systematic bribery across 12 countries. Indonesia intermediary payments documented from 1989 onwards (~GBP 2.25M); full global bribery spend across 12 countries not itemized in public record — bribery_channel_amount_usd left null to avoid anchoring MJPIS calibration on a partial figure. Markup not directly itemized; disgorged gross profit GBP 258.17M represents tainted contract profit. Reference case for UK SFO anchor in MJPIS multi-jurisdiction intersection methodology (Phase B sub-task 2.3.7).

cases.legal_basis: Failure of a commercial organisation to prevent bribery, Bribery Act 2010 s.7 (5 counts)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Airbus SE
cases.source_document: SFO v Airbus SE Approved Judgment (Dame Victoria Sharp PQBD), 2020-01-31; UK SFO DPA resolution, 5 counts s.7 Bribery Act 2010; additional source: https://www.judiciary.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Approved-Judgment-SFO-v-Airbus.pdf
cases.notes: Simultaneous three-jurisdiction resolution 31 Jan 2020: UK SFO EUR 991M (this entry), French PNF CJIP EUR 2,083M (separate entry FR_PNF_Airbus_2020), US DOJ/State USD 525M. Global resolution EUR 3.6B — largest corporate bribery resolution at time of DPA. Conduct period 2011-2015. Five countries affected by UK portion: Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Indonesia, Taiwan, Ghana. Indictment 5 counts s.7 Bribery Act 2010. UK breakdown: disgorgement EUR 585.94M + financial penalty EUR 398.03M + SFO costs EUR 6.99M. Approved by Dame Victoria Sharp PQBD. Co-anchor with French PNF entry for MJPIS cross-jurisdictional calibration. Markup percentage not itemized in public DPA record. bribery_channel_amount_usd left null because the public DPA record does not itemize per-country bribery spend in a form suitable for MJPIS anchoring.

cases.legal_basis: Convention Judiciaire d'Interet Public (CJIP) for bribery of foreign public officials under French law
cases.case_name: Parquet National Financier v. Airbus SE
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP Airbus SE, approved 2020-01-31; CJIP amount EUR 2,083,137,455; published on Agence Francaise Anticorruption (AFA) platform
cases.notes: Simultaneous three-jurisdiction resolution 31 Jan 2020: UK SFO EUR 991M (separate entry UK_SFO_Airbus_2020), French PNF CJIP EUR 2,083M (this entry), US DOJ/State USD 525M. Global resolution EUR 3.6B. Largest French CJIP at time of approval. AFA (Agence Francaise Anticorruption) monitor appointed as part of CJIP terms. Same underlying conduct as UK SFO entry. Co-anchor with UK SFO entry for MJPIS cross-jurisdictional calibration — cases UK_SFO_Airbus_2020 and FR_PNF_Airbus_2020 in corpus are the same conduct counted from two mature prosecution authorities, which is specifically what MJPIS intersection methodology needs. Do not double-count in MJPIS derivation: intersection methodology treats these as two jurisdictional data points on one underlying event, not two independent events.

cases.legal_basis: Failure of a commercial organisation to prevent bribery, Bribery Act 2010 s.7 (1 count)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Standard Bank Plc
cases.source_document: SFO v Standard Bank Plc Approved Judgment (Leveson P), 2015-11-30; first UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement; additional source: https://www.judiciary.uk/wp-content/uploads/2015/11/sfo-v-standard-bank_Final_1.pdf
cases.notes: First UK SFO DPA ever approved and first successful use of Bribery Act 2010 s.7 failure-to-prevent-bribery offence — the 'double first.' Approved 30 November 2015 by Sir Brian Leveson PQBD at Southwark Crown Court. Standard Bank Plc (now ICBC Standard Bank) held mandate to raise USD 600M sovereign note private placement for Government of Tanzania. Tanzanian subsidiary Stanbic Bank Tanzania paid USD 6M to EGMA (Enterprise Growth Market Advisors), a Tanzanian consultancy whose directors included the serving Commissioner of the Tanzania Revenue Authority (Harry Kitilya) and the former CEO of the Tanzanian Capital Markets and Securities Authority (Dr Fratern Mboya). EGMA provided no substantive service; the USD 6M was withdrawn in cash over 10 days. Penalty breakdown: USD 16.8M financial penalty to SFO + USD 8.4M disgorgement of profits + USD 6M compensation to Government of Tanzania + ~USD 1.05M interest + GBP 330K (~USD 496K) SFO costs + PwC independent compliance monitor at Standard Bank expense. Total approximately USD 32.7M. Standard Bank self-reported to SFO within 4 weeks of internal investigation commencement, which was material to Sir Brian Leveson's approval. Bribery Act 2010 s.7 template case for all subsequent UK DPAs. Bribery-channel dimensional anchor for MJPIS: documented channel amount USD 6M, underlying contract value USD 600M, channel ratio 1.0%. First corpus case where both bribery channel amount and underlying contract value are public and explicit, making this the bribery-channel dimensional anchor that Rolls-Royce and Airbus cannot provide (those cases are sustained-scheme with no per-transaction channel itemization in the public record).

cases.legal_basis: Corruption d'agent public etranger (bribery of foreign public official), French Penal Code; Convention Judiciaire d'Interet Public (CJIP) under Sapin II law
cases.case_name: Parquet National Financier v. Bollore SE and Financiere de l'Odet SE
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP Bollore SE and Financiere de l'Odet SE, CJIP signed 2021-02-09, corporate CJIP approved 2021-02-26 by Paris Tribunal Judiciaire (Judge Isabelle Prevost-Desprez); total penalty EUR 12,000,000 (~USD 14.5M historical); additional source: https://www.asso-sherpa.org/corruption-in-togo-french-supreme-court-rejected-annulation-of-proceedings-requested-by-vincent-bollore-and-others
cases.notes: Seventh French CJIP. Signed 9 Feb 2021 between PNF and Bollore SE + Financiere de l'Odet SE; corporate CJIP approved 26 Feb 2021 by Paris Tribunal Judiciaire (Judge Isabelle Prevost-Desprez) at EUR 12M fine. Underlying conduct: Euro RSCG (Bollore subsidiary, now Havas) provided communications and consulting services for President Faure Gnassingbe's 2010 Togolese presidential campaign at undercharged rates, in exchange for port concession extensions at Lome port (operated by Bollore Africa Logistics subsidiary). Parallel Guinea proceedings (Alpha Conde 2010 presidential campaign, ~EUR 170,000 in undercharged communications services in exchange for Conakry port concession) dropped on prescription. CJIP allowed Bollore to escape exclusion from public procurement, which was the most consequential institutional sanction available — this is a notable structural data point on how French negotiated justice handles procurement-debarment trade-offs and matters for SUNLIGHT's positioning on the difference between financial penalty and procurement-eligibility consequences. Same-day CRPC plea agreements for three executives (Vincent Bollore, Gilles Alix, Jean-Philippe Dorent, EUR 375,000 each) were REJECTED by the same court on 26 Feb 2021 as inappropriate for the gravity of the conduct; trial scheduled 7-17 December 2026 at Paris Tribunal Correctionnel. Procurement-bribery dimensional category: port concession contracts as the procurement instrument, political campaign services as the bribery channel. Distinct from Airbus FR PNF entry (case 12), which was aircraft sales bribery via intermediaries — this is procurement-concession bribery via in-kind services. Bribery channel amount left null because the public CJIP record describes 'undercharging' rather than itemizing a specific channel transfer for the Togolese conduct. Resolution type: CJIP (French equivalent of DPA); total penalty EUR 12,000,000 (~USD 14.5M historical); countries affected: 1 (Togo, with parallel Guinea proceedings dropped on prescription).

cases.legal_basis: False accounting, Theft Act 1968 s.17
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Tesco Stores Limited
cases.source_document: SFO v Tesco Stores Limited Approved Judgment (Leveson P), 2017-04-10; approved under reporting restrictions until January 2019; additional source: https://www.sfo.gov.uk/cases/tesco-plc/
cases.notes: Fourth UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement and the first UK SFO use of a false accounting DPA. Approved 10 April 2017 by Sir Brian Leveson PQBD at Southwark Crown Court. Tesco Stores Limited (subsidiary of Tesco PLC) admitted false accounting under Theft Act 1968 s.17 for the period February to September 2014, during which UK commercial income was unlawfully recognised in earlier accounting periods than those in which it was earned. The overstatement of expected profits was initially announced at GBP 250M on 22 September 2014, later revised upward to GBP 326M. Set against the underlying UK profit base of GBP 499M for the half-year period covered by the misstatement, the documented overstatement ratio is GBP 250M / GBP 499M = 0.501. Penalty breakdown: GBP 128,992,500 financial penalty plus approximately GBP 3M SFO costs, total approximately GBP 129M (~USD 162M historical at 2017 rates). DPA approved subject to reporting restrictions until January 2019, at which point the parallel prosecutions of three former Tesco executives (Carl Rogberg, Chris Bush, John Scouler) collapsed: Bush and Scouler's trial collapsed November 2018 with the trial judge ruling no case to answer (upheld by Court of Appeal December 2018), and the SFO offered no evidence against Rogberg in January 2019. Strategic importance for SUNLIGHT corpus: first UK SFO markup_based case with a clean documented markup ratio, and the load-bearing demonstration of the item 20 MVP architectural property committed at 04352a8. Tesco's 0.501 ratio (stored as markup_percentage=50.1 to match DynCorp's percent convention) is BELOW DynCorp 2005's 0.75 floor, which means when this case enters the corpus the MJPIS intersection methodology computes UK_SFO per-jurisdiction floor at 0.501, US_DOJ per-jurisdiction floor at 0.75, and intersection (minimum / strictest) at 0.501. The markup_floor_ratio field on the MJPIS-derived GlobalParameters instance tightens from 0.75 to 0.501 with zero code change at call sites. FIN-001 Check B threshold tightens correspondingly from award_ratio > 1.75 to award_ratio > 1.501 under any profile referencing mjpis_draft_v0. Contracts with award/tender inflation between 50% and 75% that previously did not trip Check B now do, and their evidence strings cite both DynCorp 2005 and Tesco 2017 as empirical anchors via the mjpis_v0.2 intersection methodology. The DOJ profile continues to use US_FEDERAL_V0 directly (markup_floor_ratio defaults to 0.75, derivation_metadata empty), so DOJ regression is byte-identical.

cases.legal_basis: Conspiracy to corrupt (Prevention of Corruption Act 1906 s.1); Conspiracy to bribe (Bribery Act 2010 s.1); Failure to prevent bribery (Bribery Act 2010 s.7)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Sarclad Ltd
cases.source_document: SFO v Sarclad Ltd (previously 'XYZ Limited') Approved Judgment (Leveson P), 2016-07-06; reporting restrictions lifted July 2019 after individual acquittals
cases.notes: Second UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement (previously designated 'XYZ Limited' under reporting restrictions until individual acquittals July 2019). Approved 6 July 2016 by Sir Brian Leveson PQBD. Sarclad Ltd, a Yorkshire-based steel technology products supplier (subsidiary of US-registered Heico Companies group), admitted to systematic use of bribes via third-party intermediaries to secure 28 contracts in Asian jurisdictions between June 2004 and June 2012. Total contract value GBP 17.24M. Penalty: GBP 6,553,085 total (GBP 6,201,085 disgorgement of gross profits + GBP 352,000 financial penalty, the maximum cash available without rendering Sarclad insolvent). GBP 1,953,085 paid by US parent Heico as repayment of dividends received from Sarclad during the indictment period. DPA term included annual compliance reporting to SFO on third-party intermediary transactions. DPA terms have been met and the DPA has concluded. Three former executives (Michael Sorby, Adrian Leek, David Justice) were acquitted by jury at Southwark Crown Court on 16 July 2019 — the third UK SFO DPA case where the corporate accepted liability via DPA but individuals were not convicted. Sir Brian Leveson described the bribery as 'prolific', 'systematic' and 'grave'. SFO statement of facts explicitly noted the SFO was 'not able to demonstrate whether the various Sarclad agents actually paid bribes to named or unknown individuals', hence bribery channel amount left null. Gross profit margin on implicated contracts: GBP 6.2M / GBP 17.24M = 35.96%, noted as contextual data but NOT tagged markup_based because this is gross profit margin on bribery-procured contracts, not a documented overstatement ratio comparable to DynCorp or Tesco's markup_based cases. No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation. 24 of 28 implicated contracts pre-dated the Bribery Act 2010; only 4 post-dated it, and in 2 of those the agreement to pay was concluded before 1 July 2011 when the Act came into force.

cases.legal_basis: Conspiracy to make corrupt payments (Criminal Law Act 1977 s.1); Failure to prevent bribery (Bribery Act 2010 s.7)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Guralp Systems Ltd
cases.source_document: SFO v Guralp Systems Ltd Approved Judgment, 2019-10-22; reporting restrictions lifted December 2019 after individual acquittals; DPA breach enforcement proceedings November 2024; High Court enforceability confirmation January 2026
cases.notes: Sixth UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement, approved 22 October 2019 (not made public until individual acquittals December 2019). Guralp Systems Ltd, a UK seismological instrument manufacturer. Accepted charges of conspiracy to make corrupt payments and failure to prevent bribery in relation to payments to Dr. Heon-Cheol Chi, a public official at the Korea Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources (KIGAM), South Korea. Dr. Chi held various positions at KIGAM 2002-2015, agreed to recommend GSL's services and influence contract awards in exchange for corrupt payments. Total corrupt payments approximately GBP 1M over 12 years (2003-2015), in return for approximately GBP 2M worth of contracts. Approximate bribery channel ratio: GBP 1M / GBP 2M = 50%. Penalty: GBP 2,069,861 disgorgement of gross profits ONLY — no financial penalty element. First UK SFO DPA with no penalty, due to Guralp's impecunious financial circumstances (the court noted this does not set precedent). Three employees (Dr. Cansun Guralp founding director, Natalie Pearce, Andrew Bell) acquitted by jury at Southwark Crown Court December 2019 — fourth consecutive UK SFO DPA where individuals were not convicted. Dr. Chi was convicted in the US in July 2017 for money laundering the proceeds of bribes from GSL. Guralp self-reported to both SFO and US DOJ in October 2015; US DOJ issued declination letter August 2018 citing FCPA violations but ceding primacy to SFO. DPA BREACH alleged by SFO November 2024; High Court confirmed DPA enforceable January 2026 — this is a live enforcement case as of April 2026. Settlement amount USD is approximate historical conversion of GBP 2,069,861. Dimensional category: bribery_channel + sustained_scheme. NOT tagged markup_based — the 50% ratio is bribery-channel payments as a fraction of contract value, not a documented overstatement ratio, so it has no effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation. The dimensional partition in derive_mjpis_parameters() correctly routes this case to the bribery_channel pool, which has no consumer rule yet (deferred past item 20 MVP).

cases.legal_basis: Fraud (3 counts, Fraud Act 2006); False accounting (2 counts, Theft Act 1968 s.17)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Serco Geografix Ltd
cases.source_document: SFO v Serco Geografix Ltd Approved Judgment (William Davis J), 2019-07-04
cases.notes: Fifth UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement, approved 4 July 2019 by Mr Justice William Davis at Southwark Crown Court. Serco Geografix Ltd (SGL), a dormant wholly-owned subsidiary of Serco Group plc. SGL admitted three offences of fraud and two of false accounting arising from a scheme to dishonestly mislead the Ministry of Justice (MoJ) about the true extent of profits being made by parent company Serco Limited (SL) from electronic monitoring contracts between 2010 and 2013. SL's contracts with MoJ were based on a projected 14% profit margin; SL far exceeded this margin and SGL fabricated cost figures to prevent the MoJ from exercising its contractual right to an abatement of charges on unanticipated cost efficiencies. Penalty: GBP 19.2M financial penalty (50% discount from GBP 38.4M starting point for self-reporting and cooperation) + GBP 3.7M SFO costs = GBP 22.9M total (~USD 29.5M). Additionally, Serco paid GBP 12.8M compensation to MoJ as part of a GBP 70M civil settlement in 2013. Novel institutional feature: Serco Group plc (ultimate parent) provided formal undertaking guaranteeing SGL's performance under the DPA, because SGL was dormant — Mr Justice Davis noted that without these parent company undertakings 'it is very unlikely that the goals of a DPA could have been achieved.' Two former Serco directors were subsequently prosecuted but acquitted in April 2021 when the SFO failed to disclose key evidence — fifth consecutive UK SFO DPA where the corporate accepted liability but individuals were not convicted. The court described the conduct as having 'a serious impact on the integrity of the process whereby public functions are contracted out by HM Government.' NOT tagged markup_based because the underlying conduct is profit concealment through false cost reporting, not contract-price overstatement in the DynCorp/Tesco sense. NOT tagged bribery_channel because no bribes were paid to secure the contracts — the fraud was in the performance and reporting phase, not the award phase. No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation. Relevant to SUNLIGHT as institutional intelligence on government contract fraud patterns in the electronic monitoring sector.

cases.legal_basis: Corruption d'agents publics étrangers (bribery of foreign public officials); Convention Judiciaire d'Intérêt Public (CJIP) under Sapin II law
cases.case_name: PNF v. Société Générale S.A.
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP Société Générale S.A., 2018-06-04; parallel US DOJ press release 2018-06-04 (EDNY)
cases.notes: Third French PNF CJIP in the corpus. Signed and approved 4 June 2018 by Paris Tribunal de Grande Instance. Société Générale S.A. and wholly-owned subsidiary SGA Société Générale Acceptance N.V. Amount: EUR 250.15M (~USD 292.78M). Historic institutional significance: this was the FIRST-EVER coordinated resolution between US DOJ and French PNF in a foreign bribery case, establishing the tri-jurisdictional cooperation template that Airbus 2020 later scaled to include UK SFO. Underlying conduct 2004-2009: Société Générale paid over USD 90M to a Libyan intermediary ('broker') who then paid high-level Libyan officials to secure 13 investments and 1 restructuring for Société Générale from Libyan state-owned financial institutions including the Libyan Investment Authority (LIA), Libya's sovereign wealth fund. Total investment flow: approximately USD 3.66B. Total profits earned by Société Générale: approximately USD 523M. Intermediary commissions: 1.5% to 3% of each transaction nominal value. Bribery channel ratio: USD 90M / USD 3.66B = 2.46% of investment flow, or USD 90M / USD 523M = 17.2% of profit. Parallel US DOJ FCPA resolution on SAME underlying conduct: USD 585.55M FCPA total criminal penalty, with USD 292.78M credit for the PNF payment (50% offset). SGA Société Générale Acceptance N.V. pleaded guilty to one count conspiracy to violate FCPA anti-bribery provisions in EDNY before Chief Judge Dora L. Irizarry. Société Générale entered three-year DPA with DOJ. Additional LIBOR manipulation penalties: USD 275M DOJ + USD 475M CFTC for rigging the London InterBank Offered Rate between May 2010 and October 2011 — these are separate conduct from the Libya bribery scheme and not relevant to SUNLIGHT's procurement integrity scope. Per Airbus-established protocol, only the FR PNF side is entered in the corpus to avoid over-weighting one underlying event across multiple jurisdictions. The DOJ penalty is documented here for institutional context but is NOT a separate corpus entry. Cross-jurisdictional anchor dimensional tag marks this as a case where the same conduct produced parallel prosecutions in mature jurisdictions — exactly the structural precedent that validates the MJPIS intersection methodology.

cases.legal_basis: Corruption d'agent public etranger (bribery of foreign public official); Convention Judiciaire d'Interet Public (CJIP) under Sapin II law; Article 180-2 Code de procedure penale
cases.case_name: PNF v. SAS Egis Avia
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP SAS Egis Avia, 2019-12-10; Agence Française Anticorruption press release
cases.notes: Fourth French PNF CJIP in the corpus. Signed and approved 10 December 2019 by the president of the Paris Tribunal de Grande Instance under Article 180-2 of the French Code of Criminal Procedure (permitting CJIPs during the course of a judicial investigation, not only in preliminary investigations). SAS Egis Avia, a subsidiary of the Egis Group specializing in airport and air traffic services. Admitted bribery of foreign public official (corruption d'agent public etranger). Underlying procurement: EUR 4M contract signed in 2008 for the modernization and equipping of the Oran airport terminal (aerogare d'Oran) in Algeria. Mechanism: a EUR ~390,000 consultancy contract signed with a shell company domiciled in the British Virgin Islands (a French Non-Cooperative State or Territory designation) that was used to funnel payments to several Algerian intermediaries who influenced the contract award. Investigators identified multiple cash payments including EUR 12,000 to Amine Zerhouni, son of former Algerian Interior Minister Noureddine Yazid Zerhouni. Penalty calculation: EUR 1,681,345 disgorgement (margin expected from the disputed contract plus commissions paid to the shell intermediary) + EUR 918,655 additional penalty (reflecting seriousness of facts, delayed cooperation at earlier stages offset by active cooperation of new management). Total: EUR 2.6M (approximately USD 2.9M at December 2019 exchange rates). Investigation originally opened 11 October 2013 after the Direction Departementale des Finances Publiques des Hauts-de-Seine filed a complaint based on suspicious consultancy fee payments identified during an accounting review of Egis Avia. Aggravating factors: delayed cooperation in early stages, seriousness of facts involving systematic bribery over a public infrastructure procurement. Mitigating factors: age of the underlying conduct (2008 contract), active cooperation of new management who took over after the conduct. 9th CJIP overall in France since the Sapin II law introduction in 2016, 5th CJIP involving the PNF. Institutional relevance for SUNLIGHT: Egis Avia is the first procurement-infrastructure case in the FR PNF portion of the corpus. Airport terminal construction bribery in a North African country is directly structurally analogous to the development-finance procurement patterns that UNDP programs and World Bank INT investigate. The shell-company-intermediary mechanism (BVI consultancy contract used to launder bribes to host-country intermediaries) is the canonical procurement-bribery structure that SUNLIGHT's TCA engine is designed to detect through MISSING-node analysis on the stakeholder dependency graph. Bribery channel ratio: EUR 390,000 shell-company consultancy / EUR 4M contract = approximately 9.75%. NOT markup_based — this is classical bribery_channel. No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation.

cases.legal_basis: Conspiracy to make corrupt payments (Criminal Law Act 1977 s.1 and Prevention of Corruption Act 1906 / Bribery Act 2010); Failure to prevent bribery (Bribery Act 2010 s.7)
cases.case_name: Serious Fraud Office v. Amec Foster Wheeler Energy Limited
cases.source_document: SFO v Amec Foster Wheeler Energy Limited Approved Judgment (HHJ Beddoe), 2021-07-01; SFO press release 2021-07-02; parallel DOJ press release 2021-06-25
cases.notes: Tenth UK SFO Deferred Prosecution Agreement, approved 1 July 2021 by HHJ Beddoe at Southwark Crown Court — deliberately timed to the 10th anniversary of the UK Bribery Act 2010 coming into force. Amec Foster Wheeler Energy Limited (AFWEL), now wholly-owned by John Wood Group PLC following the 2017 acquisition. AFWEL took responsibility for 10 offences: 9 counts of conspiracy to make corrupt payments, and 1 count of failure to prevent bribery under section 7 of the Bribery Act 2010. Underlying conduct spanned five jurisdictions over nearly 20 years (1996-2014): Nigeria, Saudi Arabia, Malaysia, India, and Brazil, all in the oil and gas sector, involving the use of corrupt third-party agents and intermediaries by the legacy Foster Wheeler business before its acquisition by Amec. UK penalty: GBP 103M total (financial penalty plus disgorgement plus costs). Parallel resolutions: DOJ three-year DPA plus SEC cease-and-desist order totaling USD 41M (reduced to approximately USD 18M direct payment after crediting UK and Brazil portions); Brazilian leniency agreements with Ministerio Publico Federal (MPF), Controladoria-Geral da Uniao (CGU), and Advocacia-Geral da Uniao (AGU). Global settlement total: approximately USD 177M (~GBP 130M+). Best-documented specific conduct is the Brazil subset: between 2011 and 2014, AFWEL conspired with an Italian sales agent affiliated with the Monaco-based intermediary (later identified in parallel proceedings as Unaoil) to pay approximately USD 1.1M in bribes to decision-makers at Petroleo Brasileiro S.A. (Petrobras) in exchange for a USD 190M contract to design the Complexo Gas-Quimico UFN-IV gas-to-chemicals complex. AFWEL earned at least USD 12.9M in profits from the Brazil contract. Bribery channel ratio for the Brazil subset: USD 1.1M / USD 190M = 0.58%. Cross-jurisdictional protocol: per Airbus-established pattern, only the UK SFO side is entered in the corpus. The US DOJ and SEC resolutions on the same underlying conduct are documented here for institutional context but are NOT separate corpus entries (US already heavily represented with 9 seed cases). The Brazilian authority leniency agreements are NOT separate entries (Brazil not a mature corpus jurisdiction at this stage). Settlement_amount_usd recorded as approximately USD 140M, representing the UK GBP 103M portion at mid-2021 exchange rates — the global USD 177M figure is documented in notes but not used as the corpus primary number to avoid double-counting with future potential DOJ/Brazil corpus entries. This case strengthens the UK SFO bribery_channel coverage with a clean ~0.58% channel ratio on a very large contract (USD 190M), complementing Standard Bank 2015's ~1% on a different sector. The Unaoil connection is institutionally significant — Unaoil was the center of a global oil-and-gas bribery network whose investigations have produced DPAs in multiple jurisdictions and whose structural pattern (Monaco-based intermediary routing bribes through third-party consultancy contracts) is exactly the MISSING-node topology that TCA is designed to detect on stakeholder dependency graphs. NOT markup_based — this is pure bribery_channel via third-party agents. No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation.

cases.legal_basis: Favoritisme / atteinte aux regles de la commande publique (favoritism and breach of public procurement rules under Article 432-14 of the French Penal Code); Recel de favoritisme (concealment of favoritism); Convention Judiciaire d'Interet Public (CJIP) under Sapin II law; Article 41-1-2 Code de procedure penale
cases.case_name: PNF v. Bouygues Bâtiment Sud Est & Linkcity Sud Est
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP Bouygues Bâtiment Sud Est & Linkcity Sud Est, signed 2023-05-15, approved 2023-05-17 by Paris Tribunal Judiciaire
cases.notes: Fifth French PNF CJIP in the corpus. Signed 15 May 2023 with Bouygues Batiment Sud Est (BBSE, a construction operator subsidiary of the Bouygues Group) and Linkcity Sud Est (a real-estate development subsidiary), validated by the president of the Paris Tribunal Judiciaire on 17 May 2023. 17th CJIP signed by the PNF since Sapin II introduction in 2016. Amount: EUR 7.964M total (BBSE EUR 6.8M + Linkcity EUR 1.164M), approximately USD 8.6M at May 2023 exchange rates. Three-year compliance monitoring program under the supervision of the French Anti-Corruption Agency (AFA) at additional cost of EUR 1.337M borne by BBSE. Underlying conduct 2015-2018: BBSE and Linkcity obtained three public procurement contracts from the Centre Hospitalier Annecy Genevois (CHANGE public hospital) in violation of public procurement rules, specifically the principle of equal treatment of bidders. The key contract: a EUR 32M marche in 2016 for construction of a soins de suite et readaptation and unite de soins longue duree facility at Seynod — awarded to BBSE despite BBSE being ranked last among bidders on both price and deadlines. A EUR 3.317M retention basin (bassin de retention) contract awarded without competitive tendering at all. In exchange, hospital decision-makers received from BBSE and Linkcity: multiple restaurant invitations (including during active calls for tenders), and concert tickets to the 'Piano a Lyon' festival during the 2016-2017 season. Investigation opened after referral from the Chambre regionale des comptes. Penalty calculation under PNF January 2023 guidelines: restitutive portion EUR 5.943M (equal to the benefits derived from the breaches) + afflictive punitive portion to reach the EUR 7.964M total, calibrated to the seriousness of the facts. Dimensional category: procurement_favoritism + bid_rigging + public_procurement_rules_violation. This is a structurally distinct pattern from the bribery_channel cases (Egis Avia, SocGen, etc.) — the inducements here were small non-cash benefits (meals, concert tickets), and the fraud mechanism was inside the public procurement award process itself, not through offshore shell companies or third-party agents. The critical structural signal is that BBSE won a EUR 32M contract while ranked LAST on both price and deadlines — this is precisely the kind of 'contract award with missing justification' anomaly that TCA's MISSING-node analysis on the stakeholder-dependency graph is designed to surface as a structural contradiction. The inducement channel ratio (inducement value / contract value) is extremely small — hospitality-grade gifts against a EUR 35M+ contract portfolio — so the bribery_channel_percentage field is left null, and a new tag 'small_inducement_channel' marks the category. NOT markup_based (no overpricing claim in the CJIP statement of facts). No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation. Institutional significance for SUNLIGHT: this case expands the FR PNF corpus into the favoritism/bid-rigging dimension, which is structurally distinct from offshore bribery and is particularly relevant to domestic public-sector procurement contexts — exactly the patterns UNDP country offices and national audit bodies face in their own domestic public procurement oversight.

cases.legal_basis: Corruption d'agent public (bribery of public officials, Article 433-1 French Penal Code); Corruption d'agent public etranger (bribery of foreign public officials, Article 435-3 French Penal Code); Convention Judiciaire d'Interet Public (CJIP) under Sapin II law; Article 41-1-2 Code de procedure penale
cases.case_name: PNF v. Airbus SE (second CJIP)
cases.source_document: PNF CJIP Airbus SE (second), signed 2022-11-17, approved 2022-11-30 by Stephane Noel, president of Paris Tribunal Judiciaire; Airbus press release 2022-11-30
cases.notes: Sixth French PNF CJIP in the corpus and the 14th PNF CJIP overall. Signed 17 November 2022 between Airbus SE and the PNF, approved 30 November 2022 by Stephane Noel, president of the Paris Tribunal Judiciaire. Fine: EUR 15,856,044 (~USD 16.5M at November 2022 exchange rates). No compliance monitorship attached — the three-year AFA monitorship from the 2020 CJIP was still in force and covered this conduct. This CJIP is procedurally described as 'an extension' of the January 2020 CJIP (EUR 3.598B global settlement, EUR 2.083B to France) covering facts that could not be addressed simultaneously in 2020 for procedural reasons. The PNF consolidated three separate judicial investigations into this CJIP. First: Libya. Sale of 12 Airbus commercial aircraft to Libyan state carrier Afriqiyah Airways in November 2006, facilitated by two intermediaries acting as influential facilitators for Libyan government officials who received commissions of EUR 2M and EUR 4M respectively. Three weeks after the contract signing, EUR 2M was transferred via complex financial structures through official Airbus agents in Thailand to intermediary Alexandre Djouhri — this investigation is connected to the alleged Libyan financing of Nicolas Sarkozy's 2007 presidential campaign. Second: Kazakhstan ('Kazakhgate' investigation opened March 2013). Framework agreement between Airbus subsidiary Eurocopter (now Airbus Helicopters) and Kazakhstan Engineering for 45 EC 145 helicopters (2009-2010), plus Astrium satellite center and 2 satellites (total Kazakhstan package including Alstom locomotives ~EUR 2B). A EUR 8.8M suspicious payment was identified during a February 2016 search of Airbus headquarters, linked to the satellite sales. Political facilitation was provided by Aymeri de Montesquiou, a French UDI senator, who organized meetings between parties with expenses covered by Eurocopter. The contracts also appear linked to a Belgian penal transaction that resolved criminal proceedings against Patokh Chodiev, a person close to President Nursultan Nazarbayev. Third: IPA intermediary (2003-2009). Commission payments to the director of a private intermediary company called IPA and his son, a former officer in the French Ministry of Defense, to facilitate negotiation and signing of six contracts in Czech Republic, Kuwait, Croatia, and Turkmenistan. The fine of EUR 15,856,044 corresponds precisely to the total amount paid by Airbus to the suspect third parties in Libya and Kazakhstan (aggravating commissions). This is substantially smaller than the 2020 fine because (a) the 2020 CJIP covered the same fraudulent scheme structurally and (b) corrective measures were already in place via the 2020 AFA monitorship. PNF considered as aggravating: the repeated nature of corrupt acts. As mitigating: the age of the facts (10-15+ years old by 2022), Airbus cooperation, and the compliance program already in operation. Dimensional category: bribery_channel + commercial_intermediary_channel + political_corruption + sustained_scheme. NOT markup_based — this is classical third-party-intermediary bribery across cross-border defense and aerospace procurement. Bribery channel ratio not computed here because the fine equals the commission total rather than a contract-price ratio. NOT tagged cross_jurisdictional_anchor (unlike Airbus 2020 which was tri-jurisdictional with DOJ and SFO) — the 2022 CJIP is a France-only follow-on settlement. Institutional significance: this case is structurally important as a demonstration that even after the largest multi-jurisdictional anti-bribery settlement in history (EUR 3.598B in 2020), additional facts from the same underlying fraudulent scheme continued to surface and required their own settlement. The pattern of 'follow-on CJIP for procedurally-severed facts from the same underlying scheme' is itself a structural feature of institutional enforcement that SUNLIGHT's case-packet generation should be aware of. No effect on MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation.

cases.legal_basis: Failure to prevent bribery (Bribery Act 2010 s.7), 7 counts. Corporate criminal conviction following guilty plea — NOT a Deferred Prosecution Agreement.
cases.case_name: R v. Petrofac Limited
cases.source_document: R v Petrofac Limited, Southwark Crown Court (Judge Deborah Taylor), guilty plea 2021-10-01, sentenced 2021-10-04; SFO press release 2021-10-04
cases.notes: Ninth UK SFO case in the corpus and the first that is a CONVICTION rather than a Deferred Prosecution Agreement — distinct evidentiary standard from the eight UK SFO DPA cases already in the corpus (Rolls-Royce, Airbus, Standard Bank, Tesco, Sarclad, Guralp, Serco, Amec Foster Wheeler). Petrofac Limited (Jersey-registered oil services company) pleaded guilty 1 October 2021 at Westminster Magistrates Court and was sentenced 4 October 2021 by Judge Deborah Taylor at Southwark Crown Court. Pleaded guilty to seven separate counts of failing to prevent bribery under section 7 of the Bribery Act 2010 — the third-ever section 7 prosecution in the courts of England and Wales. Total penalty: GBP 77M (~USD 105M). Breakdown: GBP 47.2M financial penalty + GBP 22.8M confiscation order + GBP 7M SFO costs. Underlying conduct between October 2011 and May 2017: senior executives within Petrofac Group paid GBP 32M (USD 44M) in bribes to agents to win GBP 2.6B (USD 3.5B) of oil and gas contracts in Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates. Bribery channel ratio: USD 44M / USD 3.5B = 1.26%. Specific largest contract: USD 1.7B EPC Engineering Procurement Construction contract for the Jazan Refinery and Terminal Project in Saudi Arabia, secured via USD 21.4M in series of corrupt payments. Concealment methods: payments made against false invoices, sub-contracts, fictitious services contracts, and through companies already legitimately contracted to provide services to Petrofac (layered concealment via existing legitimate vendor relationships). Judge Deborah Taylor described the corruption as 'systemic, serious and grave.' Individual executive: David Lufkin, former Global Head of Sales at Petrofac, was the first SFO cooperator whose individual conviction enabled the corporate conviction. Lufkin pleaded guilty to 14 counts of individual bribery (11 counts in February 2019 covering Iraq and Saudi Arabia, 3 additional counts in January 2021 covering UAE Upper Zakum UZ750 Field Development and Bab Integrated Facilities Projects). Lufkin's bribery total: USD 81M paid to secure USD 8.4B of contracts. Lufkin received a 2-year custodial sentence suspended for 18 months — substantial reduction from a possible 7-year sentence due to his cooperation. The SFO's investigation began 12 May 2017 and was part of the broader Unaoil network probe (Monaco-based intermediary), the same network as Amec Foster Wheeler 2021 already in the corpus. Dimensional category: bribery_channel + sustained_scheme + third_party_agent_channel + multi_country_conduct + oil_and_gas_sector + unaoil_related + criminal_conviction_not_dpa + fictitious_invoice_concealment + individual_executive_conviction. NOT markup_based. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. Institutional significance: this is the highest-evidentiary-standard UK SFO outcome in the corpus (criminal conviction at the corporate level, not just admission via DPA), strengthening the UK SFO anchor's evidentiary baseline. The fictitious-invoice and existing-vendor-channel concealment patterns are exactly the structural anomalies TCA's stakeholder dependency graph is designed to surface — payments routed through legitimate vendor relationships are the hardest topology for indicator-based methods to detect because they look like normal commercial activity. Two cases in the corpus now reference the Unaoil network (Amec Foster Wheeler 2021 and Petrofac 2021), establishing Unaoil as a structural cluster for future case-packet cross-referencing logic. NOTE on attempted case substitution: NatWest 2021 was originally targeted for this slot. NatWest's GBP 264.8M conviction is FCA-prosecuted, not SFO-prosecuted — different prosecutor, different statutory basis (Money Laundering Regulations 2007 vs. Bribery Act 2010), different evidentiary frame. Adding NatWest under the UK_SFO jurisdiction tag would mislabel jurisdictional provenance and corrupt the MJPIS partition logic. NatWest is documented as a known FCA prosecution but excluded from the UK_SFO corpus per the dimensional relevance protocol that governs Phase B extraction.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: Negotiated Resolution Agreement under the World Bank Group Sanctions System; debarment with conditional release for misconduct in violation of World Bank procurement guidelines (corrupt practices); cross-debarment under the 2010 MDB Agreement for Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. SNC-Lavalin Inc.
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2013-04-17; Negotiated Resolution Agreement between the World Bank Group and SNC-Lavalin Inc., April 2013; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings
cases.notes: FIRST WORLD BANK INT CASE IN THE CORPUS — opens the fourth MJPIS jurisdiction (alongside US DOJ, UK SFO, FR PNF). World Bank Group debarment announced 17 April 2013 via Negotiated Resolution Agreement (NRA, the World Bank Group's analogue to a Deferred Prosecution Agreement). SNC-Lavalin Inc. (Canadian engineering and construction company, subsidiary of SNC-Lavalin Group Inc., now AtkinsRealis) plus over 100 affiliates debarred for 10 years — the LONGEST debarment period that had ever been agreed to in a World Bank settlement at the time of imposition. Reducible to 8 years upon compliance with all conditions of the agreement (this reduction was ultimately granted: sanction lifted April 2021 after 8 years served). Underlying conduct concerns TWO World Bank-financed projects. Project 1: Padma Multipurpose Bridge Project in Bangladesh — a 6.15 km road-rail bridge across the Padma River intended to connect southwest Bangladesh to northern and eastern regions, originally to be financed by a World Bank loan of approximately USD 1.2B. SNC-Lavalin engaged in a conspiracy to pay bribes to Bangladesh government officials in exchange for influence over the award of the Padma supervision consultancy contract (the contract for which SNC-Lavalin was bidding). The World Bank's Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) found that SNC-Lavalin executives had developed a 'tick list' of officials to be bribed and had budgeted bribe amounts. The misconduct led the World Bank to withdraw entirely from the Padma Bridge project in June 2012 — Bangladesh ultimately self-financed the bridge, completing it in 2022 at a cost overrun of over USD 3B and a four-year schedule slip. Project 2: Rural Electrification and Transmission Project in Cambodia — discovered by INT during the Padma investigation. Similar misconduct pattern. Sanctions structure: NO financial penalty (World Bank INT sanctions are administrative, not monetary). The sanction is debarment from bidding on or receiving any World Bank-financed contract for the debarment period. Cross-debarment under the 2010 Agreement for Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions among the African Development Bank, Asian Development Bank, European Bank for Reconstruction and Development, Inter-American Development Bank, and World Bank Group means SNC-Lavalin was simultaneously debarred from procurement at all five MDBs. The 'remainder of the SNC-Lavalin Group' (parent company and other subsidiaries not directly implicated) was 'conditionally non-debarred' for the same period, meaning compliance failures by the parent would have re-triggered debarment. Settlement amount: USD 0 (no monetary penalty applies to WB INT sanctions). Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + sustained_scheme + procurement_infrastructure + bridge_project + rural_electrification + multilateral_development_bank_sanction + cross_debarment_eligible + longest_wb_debarment_at_time_of_imposition. NOT markup_based — this is bribery to win bids on multilateral-financed infrastructure procurement, not contract-price overstatement. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio derivation: the 4th jurisdiction is now in the corpus; the existing dimensional partition logic (markup-floor pool requires 'markup_based' tag) correctly excludes SNC-Lavalin from the markup floor computation. markup_floor_ratio should remain pinned at 0.501 (DynCorp 2005 + Tesco 2017). The non-DOJ case count grows from 15 to 16, jurisdictions_considered grows from 3 to 4 (FR_PNF, UK_SFO, US_DOJ, WB_INT). HISTORIC SIGNIFICANCE: This case is the canonical reference example for World Bank INT enforcement of procurement integrity in development-finance contexts. SNC-Lavalin Padma is to World Bank INT what DynCorp is to US DOJ and what Rolls-Royce is to UK SFO — the high-profile, high-precedent, well-documented anchor case that defines the jurisdiction's enforcement posture. Specifically relevant to SUNLIGHT's UNDP integration: UNDP procurement is structurally analogous to World Bank-financed procurement, and the bribery topology surfaced by INT in the Padma case (multinational engineering firm bidding on multilateral-financed infrastructure consultancy contract via bribery to host-country officials) is precisely the topology UNDP investigators face in their own pre-award verification work. The administrative_sanctionable dimension introduced by this case is the future consumer surface for a SUNLIGHT engine module that would compute MJPIS thresholds for debarment-grade findings (distinct from criminal-grade and DPA-grade findings already covered). Future MJPIS development can now compute administrative-sanctionable thresholds across a multi-jurisdictional corpus once 3+ World Bank INT cases are populated.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: Negotiated Resolution Agreement under the World Bank Group Sanctions System; debarment with conditional release for misconduct in violation of World Bank procurement guidelines (corrupt practices); restitution payment under NRA terms; cross-debarment under the 2010 MDB Agreement for Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. Alstom Hydro France / Alstom Network Schweiz AG
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2012-02-22; Negotiated Resolution Agreement between the World Bank Group and Alstom Hydro France / Alstom Network Schweiz AG, February 2012; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings
cases.notes: Second World Bank INT case in the corpus and the FIRST WB INT case in the corpus with a monetary restitution component. Announced 22 February 2012 by the World Bank Group via Negotiated Resolution Agreement. Alstom Hydro France and Alstom Network Schweiz AG (Switzerland) plus their affiliates debarred for THREE YEARS, reducible to 21 months upon compliance with all conditions of the agreement and enhanced oversight. Alstom SA (the parent company) and its other affiliates conditionally non-debarred for the same period. Underlying conduct: in 2002, Alstom made an improper payment of EUR 110,000 to an entity controlled by a former senior government official of Zambia, characterized in the corrupt scheme as payment for 'consultancy services' but actually used to influence procurement decisions, in connection with the World Bank-financed Zambia Power Rehabilitation Project (hydropower sector). Restitution payment: USD 9.5M total paid by Alstom Hydro France and Alstom Network Schweiz AG combined — substantial monetary penalty distinct from the SNC-Lavalin 2013 case which carried zero monetary penalty. The restitution funds were directed to the project beneficiaries (development restitution mechanism, conceptually similar to the Lotti Indonesia precedent of 2010 where USD 350K restitution was paid to Indonesia following a water-sector misconduct case). Cross-debarment under the 2010 Agreement for Mutual Recognition of Debarments extended the sanction to AfDB, ADB, EBRD, IDB. NOTE on broader Alstom enforcement: This World Bank INT case is conduct-distinct from the December 2014 US DOJ FCPA settlement against Alstom S.A. (USD 772M, the largest FCPA penalty against a single company at that time, covering bribery in Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Bahamas, and Taiwan in the power and rail sectors — that DOJ case is NOT in this corpus entry and would be a separate US DOJ case if added). The 2002 Zambia conduct documented here was identified by INT through its independent investigation channel and resolved through the WB sanctions system, not through cross-referral from US DOJ. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + procurement_infrastructure + hydropower_project + multilateral_development_bank_sanction + cross_debarment_eligible + restitution_payment + former_senior_official_intermediary. The 'former_senior_official_intermediary' tag captures the structural pattern of using a former high-level official's controlled entity as the bribe channel — a common topology distinct from third-party-agent bribery or shell-company channels because it leverages residual political access of someone formally outside the current government structure. NOT markup_based. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio must remain 0.501. Institutional significance: this case is the second-most-cited WB INT precedent (after SNC-Lavalin Padma) and establishes the WB INT enforcement template for the European multinational engineering sector. Restitution mechanism is structurally important — the WB INT NRA system can carry monetary penalties when the restitution would benefit the affected developing country, distinguishing it from purely administrative debarment-only sanctions.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: comprehensive global settlement agreement with the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD), the International Development Association (IDA), the International Finance Corporation (IFC), and the Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA); voluntary restraint from bidding plus debarment of named subsidiary; restitutive USD 100M payment to anti-corruption initiatives; voluntary disclosure obligations and cooperation undertakings; cross-debarment foundational case for the April 2010 MDB Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions Agreement
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. Siemens AG
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2009-07-02; Settlement Agreement between the World Bank Group and Siemens AG, July 2009; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings; OOO Siemens debarment notice 2009-11-30
cases.notes: Third WB INT case in the corpus. Comprehensive global settlement agreement entered 2 July 2009 between Siemens AG (Berlin and Munich; one of the world's largest electronics and electrical engineering companies, ~405,000 employees worldwide as of fiscal 2009) and the World Bank Group as a unified party comprising all four operational institutions: International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD), International Development Association (IDA), International Finance Corporation (IFC), and Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA). This is the multi-institutional structural feature that distinguishes the Siemens settlement from typical WB INT debarments which are usually administered by INT alone. Three structural components: (1) Voluntary restraint: Siemens AG and ALL of its consolidated subsidiaries and affiliates voluntarily refrained from bidding on any World Bank Group-financed project for two years, from 1 January 2009 through 31 December 2010. Bids not yet accepted as of January 2009 had to be withdrawn. (2) Subsidiary debarment: separately on 30 November 2009, OOO Siemens (Limited Liability Company Siemens Russia, the Russian subsidiary of Siemens AG) and all its controlled subsidiaries were debarred for four years following an INT investigation into fraudulent and corrupt practices under the Bank-financed Moscow Urban Transport Project (conduct period prior to 2007). OOO Siemens did not contest the debarment. (3) Restitutive payment: Siemens committed to pay USD 100M over 15 years to anti-corruption organizations and projects worldwide, structured through the Siemens Integrity Initiative launched 9 December 2009. The Initiative awarded grants to over 30 projects across 20+ countries in its first funding round (~USD 40M committed); a second funding round opened in December 2014. Beneficiary categories: collective action initiatives, training and education, asset recovery support for governments recovering assets stolen by corrupt leaders, and identification/cracking down on corrupt practices. (4) Compliance and disclosure obligations: Siemens engaged Dr. Theo Waigel, former German federal finance minister, as compliance monitor for up to four years (this monitorship was concurrent with the US DOJ/SEC monitorship from the December 2008 USD 1.6B FCPA settlement); Siemens also accepted a voluntary disclosure obligation requiring it to report any potential misconduct on Bank Group Projects to INT going forward. (5) Industry collaboration: Siemens agreed to cooperate to change industry practices, clean up procurement practices, and engage in collective action with the WBG to fight fraud and corruption. Settlement amount: USD 100M recorded in this corpus entry as the restitutive payment component (analogous to a non-fine monetary component). Note that this is structurally distinct from a DOJ/SFO fine — the USD 100M is paid to anti-corruption organizations rather than to the WBG itself or to a victim. The restitutive nature complicates direct comparison with markup-based or bribery-channel monetary metrics. CROSS-DEBARMENT FOUNDATIONAL: the Siemens settlement was the catalyst for the April 2010 Agreement for Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions among the five Multilateral Development Banks (African Development Bank, Asian Development Bank, European Bank for Reconstruction and Development, Inter-American Development Bank, and World Bank Group). The agreement established that companies debarred by one MDB could no longer seek business from any other MDB. The cross-debarment regime that SNC-Lavalin 2013 (case 25) was sanctioned under directly traces its institutional origin to the Siemens settlement architecture. Institutional significance: the Siemens 2009 case is the canonical WB INT enforcement reference event — the high-profile, precedent-setting anchor that defines the World Bank Group's modern enforcement posture. Structurally analogous to DynCorp 2005 for US DOJ and Rolls-Royce 2017 for UK SFO. The corpus now contains the full institutional foundation for WB INT enforcement. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + sustained_scheme + voluntary_disclosure_agreement + multi_institutional_settlement + cross_debarment_foundational + anti_corruption_initiative_funding + global_corporate_shutout + named_subsidiary_debarment + moscow_urban_transport_project + wb_int_anchor_case. NOT markup_based. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501 (DynCorp + Tesco binding pair). With this case, WB INT reaches 3 cases in the corpus, the threshold above which Phase C derivation upgrades can begin computing WB INT-specific dimensional thresholds for any future consumer modules (bribery_channel, administrative_sanctionable). Note on FCPA parallel: in December 2008, Siemens AG also entered settlements with US DOJ (USD 800M) and SEC (USD 350M) and with Munich public prosecutors (~EUR 1B), totaling approximately USD 1.6B in FCPA-related penalties — at the time the largest FCPA settlement in history. Per cross-jurisdictional protocol, the FCPA side is documented here for institutional context but is NOT a separate corpus entry under US DOJ (the US DOJ corpus is anchored by its own seed cases and Siemens FCPA was 2008, predating the WB INT settlement that the WB INT entry covers). Future architectural consideration: if the corpus eventually expands US DOJ to cover FCPA-anchor cases as well as the existing seed set, Siemens FCPA 2008 would be a natural addition under that arc.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: Negotiated Resolution Agreement; debarment with conditional release for sanctionable conduct (corrupt practices) in violation of World Bank procurement guidelines under the Sudan Multi-Donor Trust Fund; cross-debarment eligible under the April 2010 MDB Mutual Enforcement Agreement
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. Macmillan Limited
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2010-04-30; Negotiated Resolution Agreement between the World Bank Group and Macmillan Limited, April 2010; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings
cases.notes: Fourth WB INT case in the corpus. World Bank Group debarment announced 30 April 2010. Macmillan Limited (UK educational publisher, at the time part of the German Verlagsgruppe Georg von Holtzbrinck GmbH group). 6-year debarment from being awarded Bank-financed contracts, reduced from a baseline 8-year term in recognition of early acknowledgement of misconduct. Further reducible to 3 years upon implementation of a compliance program and continued cooperation with INT (this further reduction was ultimately granted; Macmillan was released from debarment in subsequent years). Underlying conduct 2008-2009: Macmillan Education (a subsidiary of Macmillan Limited) paid bribes to a local official in Southern Sudan attempting to influence the award of a multi-million pound contract to publish school textbooks under the Sudan Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) education rehabilitation project. The MDTF was established in 2006 to fund post-conflict reconstruction in Southern Sudan and is administered by the World Bank. STRUCTURALLY DISTINCT FEATURE: the bribery was UNSUCCESSFUL — Macmillan paid bribes but did NOT win the contract. This is the first case in the corpus where the underlying corrupt conduct failed to produce its intended procurement outcome. The 'attempted bribery / failed to win' pattern is institutionally significant because it expands the corpus beyond the dominant 'successful bribery / won contract' topology and provides a data point for the structural class of attempted-but-thwarted corruption that institutional integrity systems must also detect and deter. SECTOR DIVERSITY: education/publishing sector, distinct from the infrastructure (SNC-Lavalin Padma Bridge, Alstom Hydro Zambia) and electronics/transportation (Siemens Moscow Urban Transport) WB INT cases already in the corpus. The educational textbook procurement context is directly relevant to UNDP's broader development-finance footprint, where post-conflict reconstruction procurement (similar to the Sudan MDTF context) is a recurring institutional setting. SELF-REPORTED: Macmillan voluntarily disclosed the misconduct to the World Bank, contributing to the reduction from baseline. Macmillan also voluntarily disclosed the matter to the UK Serious Fraud Office. CROSS-PROSECUTOR REFERRAL ESTABLISHED: based on the INT referral, UK authorities subsequently ordered Macmillan Publishers Limited to pay over GBP 11 million in UK courts. This case is the canonical reference example of the WB INT-to-national-prosecutor referral pattern that underpins the global anti-corruption enforcement network — INT investigates, refers to national authority, national authority pursues criminal/civil action with the WB INT evidentiary record as foundation. The same pattern subsequently applied to the WB INT-Norwegian Oekokrim referral on Norconsult and others. Cross-debarment eligibility: the Macmillan debarment qualified for cross-debarment under the April 2010 Agreement for Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions (which entered into force just three weeks before the Macmillan debarment was announced), making Macmillan one of the early cases to test the cross-debarment regime that Siemens 2009 had structurally precipitated. Compliance obligations: Macmillan committed to implement a compliance monitoring program and engage a compliance monitor; established a board-level Risk and Compliance Committee; conducted a review of Macmillan Education's practices and procedures; began implementation of a revised ethical framework with comprehensive risk assessment and staff training. Settlement amount: USD 0 in the corpus entry, consistent with WB INT sanctions structure (administrative debarment, not monetary). The downstream GBP 11M UK court order is documented in case notes for institutional context but is NOT a separate corpus entry under UK_SFO because (a) it was a UK court order following INT referral, not an SFO DPA or conviction, and (b) entering it would double-count the same underlying conduct against the dimensional_tag analysis. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + unsuccessful_bribery_attempt + education_publishing_sector + self_reported + cross_debarment_eligible + int_referral_to_national_prosecutor + sudan_mdtf + post_conflict_reconstruction_context. NOT markup_based. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. WB INT now contains 4 cases spanning 4 sectors (infrastructure bridges, infrastructure hydropower, electronics/transportation, education/publishing), 2 outcome classes (successful and unsuccessful bribery), and 2 monetary structures (no monetary component, monetary restitution component). The WB INT slice of the corpus is now dimensionally diverse enough to support meaningful Phase C derivation work on the administrative_sanctionable and bribery_channel dimensions.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: Negotiated Resolution Agreement; 18-month debarment of Alcatel-Lucent Trade International A.G. and Alcatel Saudi Arabia Limited for acknowledged sanctionable practices under the World Bank-financed Emergency Private Sector Development Project in Iraq; cross-debarment eligible under the April 2010 MDB Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions Agreement
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. Alcatel-Lucent Trade International A.G. and Alcatel Saudi Arabia Limited
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2015-04-23; Negotiated Resolution Agreement between the World Bank Group and Alcatel-Lucent Trade International A.G. / Alcatel Saudi Arabia Limited, April 2015; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings
cases.notes: Fifth WB INT case in the corpus. Debarment announced 23 April 2015 by the World Bank Group via Negotiated Resolution Agreement. Two entities debarred for 18 months: Alcatel-Lucent Trade International A.G. (Swiss trading entity) and Alcatel Saudi Arabia Limited (Saudi-incorporated subsidiary). The 'Saudi Arabia' in the entity name reflects corporate incorporation, not the conduct jurisdiction — the underlying misconduct occurred in Iraq. Underlying project: World Bank-financed Emergency Private Sector Development Project in the Republic of Iraq. Conduct: acknowledged sanctionable practices (the WB press release does not specify whether the finding was corrupt practice, fraudulent practice, or both — 'sanctionable practices' is the WB category-agnostic framing; inferred as corrupt practice given the parallel DOJ FCPA proceeding). Sanction duration: 18 months, reflecting significant mitigation credit for acknowledgment of sanctionable practices and cooperation with the INT investigation. Structurally, this is the SHORTEST WB INT debarment in the SUNLIGHT corpus — the prior four WB INT cases were 6 years (Macmillan 2010), 10 years reduced to 8 (SNC-Lavalin 2013), unknown multi-year period (Alstom Hydro 2012), and 2-year voluntary shutout + 4-year subsidiary debarment (Siemens 2009). The 18-month duration data point expands the corpus's duration distribution for WB INT, which is directly relevant to any future Phase C derivation work on the administrative_sanctionable dimension. PARALLEL DOJ PROCEEDING: in December 2010, Alcatel-Lucent S.A. (parent) entered a DOJ DPA and SEC consent judgment totaling USD 137M for FCPA violations spanning Costa Rica, Honduras, Taiwan, and Malaysia. Two Alcatel-Lucent subsidiaries (Alcatel CIT and Alcatel-Lucent Trade International A.G.) pleaded guilty. The DOJ resolution included a commitment to share facts with debarment authorities, which directly enabled the WB INT proceeding that culminated in this 2015 debarment — the 2015 debarment is the WB's follow-on enforcement on a distinct World Bank-financed project (Iraq), not duplicating the countries covered by DOJ. Per cross-jurisdictional protocol, only the WB INT side is entered in the corpus. The DOJ 2010 FCPA resolution is documented here for institutional context but is NOT a separate US_DOJ corpus entry. Telecommunications sector is new to the WB INT corpus slice: prior WB INT cases covered infrastructure bridges (SNC-Lavalin), infrastructure hydropower (Alstom), electronics/transportation (Siemens), and education/publishing (Macmillan). Telecommunications procurement in post-conflict reconstruction contexts (Iraq Emergency Private Sector Development) is directly analogous to the procurement categories UNDP programs in countries emerging from conflict. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + telecommunications_sector + iraq_emergency_project + short_duration_debarment + cross_debarment_eligible + wb_doj_parallel_track + emergency_private_sector_development. NOT markup_based. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. Settlement amount: USD 0 in corpus entry, consistent with WB INT sanctions structure (administrative debarment, not monetary). Institutional significance: this case demonstrates the WB-DOJ cooperation pattern in practice — DOJ resolves FCPA conduct in countries A, B, C, D; WB INT follows up on a distinct Bank-financed project in country E; both enforcement tracks reinforce each other without overlapping. For SUNLIGHT's UNDP positioning, this pattern shows that WB INT's enforcement reach extends beyond the specific countries covered by criminal prosecutors and captures procurement-integrity risk across the full WB-financed project portfolio. WB INT now contains 5 cases spanning 5 sectors (infrastructure bridges, infrastructure hydropower, electronics/transportation, education/publishing, telecommunications) and duration range from 18 months to 10 years.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: debarment following WB Sanctions Board determination of collusive bid-rigging practices under Phase 1 of the Philippines National Roads Improvement and Management Program (NRIMP 1, WB-financed USD 150M loan); WB Procurement Guidelines Section 1.14 (fraud and corruption); multi-entity sanction covering seven firms and one individual; cross-debarment eligible; successor-liability doctrine extended 2011 to CCCC as designated successor entity
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. China Road and Bridge Corporation et al. (NRIMP 1 Philippines cartel)
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2009-01-14; World Bank Group press release 2011-07-29 (CCCC successor-liability extension); World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings; WB Sanctions Board decision
cases.notes: Sixth WB INT case in the corpus and the FIRST CARTEL/COLLUSIVE-BID-RIGGING case in the corpus across all jurisdictions. Sanctions announced 14 January 2009 by the World Bank Group following investigation by the Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) and determination by the WB Sanctions Board that seven firms and one individual engaged in a collusive bid-rigging scheme on Phase 1 of the Philippines National Roads Improvement and Management Program (NRIMP 1), a USD 150M WB-financed roads rehabilitation project that built or resurfaced 1,400 km of roads in the Philippines. The cartel affected at least USD 30-45M of project funds; WB intervention prevented an estimated USD 33M in further fund awards to sanctioned entities. This case is recorded in the corpus as a SINGLE ENTRY under case_id WB_INT_CRBC_NRIMP1_2009 representing the anchor entity (China Road and Bridge Corporation) and the full cartel event. Per cross-jurisdictional and anti-double-counting protocol, the corpus does not add separate entries for each of the seven firms — a single cartel event is counted once as a structural sanctions episode, with all sanctioned parties enumerated in these notes. COMPLETE SANCTIONED PERIMETER (January 2009 sanctions unless noted otherwise): (1) China Road and Bridge Corp (CRBC, Beijing) — 8 YEARS — headline entity, designated successor to becomes CCCC in 2006 merger; (2) China State Construction Engineering Corp (Beijing) — 6 years; (3) China Wu Yi Co. Ltd. (Fuzhou) — 6 years; (4) China Geo-Engineering Corp (Beijing) — 5 years; (5) Cavite Ideal International Construction and Development Corp (Pasay City, Philippines) — 4 years; (6) CM Pancho Construction Inc. (Quezon City, Philippines) — 4 years; (7) E.C. de Luna Construction Corp (Manila) — PERMANENTLY DEBARRED — first permanent-debarment data point in the SUNLIGHT corpus; (8) Eduardo C. de Luna (individual, owner of E.C. de Luna) — PERMANENTLY DEBARRED — first individual permanent-debarment data point. Separately, Dongsung Construction Co. Ltd. (Gyeongnam, Korea) was debarred 4 years in August 2008 for fraudulent and corrupt practices in the same NRIMP 1 case (pre-dating the main January 2009 debarment announcement by approximately 5 months). SUCCESSOR-LIABILITY EXTENSION: on 29 July 2011, the World Bank extended the CRBC 8-year debarment to China Communications Construction Company (CCCC) Limited and all its subsidiaries — CCCC is the designated successor entity to CRBC following the 2006 merger that created CCCC from CRBC + China Harbour Engineering Company. The successor-liability extension was based on recent changes to the WB sanctions system clarifying that successor organizations (through purchase or reorganization) are subject to the same sanctions as the original firm. Extension rendered CCCC and subsidiaries ineligible for WB-financed road and bridge projects until 12 January 2017. This is the FIRST SUCCESSOR-LIABILITY EXTENSION DOCTRINE APPLICATION in the SUNLIGHT corpus. The doctrine is institutionally important because it prevents sanctioned entities from evading debarment through corporate reorganization, name changes, or successor-entity restructuring — a concern directly relevant to UNDP's vendor re-registration loophole identified in the 2024 OAI annual report. DIMENSIONAL NOVELTY: this case introduces three structural patterns new to the WB INT corpus slice and to the overall corpus: (a) FIRST CARTEL/COLLUSIVE-BID-RIGGING case — distinct from individual-company bribery topology. The cartel operated across Chinese SOEs and Philippine domestic construction firms. Reports cited systemic corruption involving bureaucrats, politicians, and media/NGO recipients of bribe shares. The cartel had 'operated with impunity for at least a decade'; (b) FIRST PERMANENT DEBARMENT in corpus (E.C. de Luna + Eduardo C. de Luna individually); (c) FIRST SUCCESSOR-LIABILITY EXTENSION in corpus. SECTOR NOVELTY: roads infrastructure joins the WB INT sector coverage, which now spans infrastructure bridges (SNC-Lavalin), infrastructure hydropower (Alstom), electronics/transportation (Siemens), education/publishing (Macmillan), telecommunications (Alcatel-Lucent), and now roads infrastructure (CRBC cartel). Settlement amount: USD 0 in corpus entry (WB INT sanctions are administrative, no monetary penalty); the $33M in prevented fund awards and the $30-45M of project funds at risk are documented as institutional-context scale figures but not as settlement amounts. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + collusive_bid_rigging + multi_entity_cartel + roads_infrastructure_sector + philippines_nrimp1 + permanent_debarment_present + successor_liability_extension + cross_debarment_eligible + wb_sanctions_board_decision + wb_int_anchor_case_for_cartel_pattern. NOT markup_based. NOT bribery_channel in the classical offshore-shell sense (the mechanism here was horizontal collusion among bidders, not vertical bribery of officials — though vertical bribery was present in the broader NRIMP 1 ecosystem per INT findings). MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. WB INT slice now contains 6 cases spanning 6 sectors, duration range from 18 months to permanent, and now covering three distinct conduct topologies (individual bribery, individual fraud, multi-entity cartel). Institutional significance for SUNLIGHT: the cartel pattern is directly analogous to the procurement-favoritism pattern in the FR PNF Bouygues/Linkcity 2023 case (horizontal coordination among bidders and/or contracting authority preference, rather than offshore bribery), establishing structural parallels between WB INT cartel cases and FR PNF favoritism cases that future Phase C derivation work on the collusive_bid_rigging / procurement_favoritism dimensions can leverage.

cases.legal_basis: World Bank Group sanctions: Negotiated Resolution Agreement; 3-year debarment of Oxford University Press East Africa Limited (OUPEA) and Oxford University Press Tanzania Limited (OUPT) for acknowledged corrupt practices (improper payments to government officials) in relation to two Bank-financed education projects in East Africa; conditional non-debarment of OUP parent; USD 500,000 restitution payment to the World Bank; cross-debarment eligible under April 2010 MDB Mutual Enforcement of Debarment Decisions Agreement; parallel UK SFO High Court civil recovery action against Oxford Publishing Limited under Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 resulting in GBP 1,895,435 order plus voluntary GBP 2,000,000 contribution to sub-Saharan Africa educational charities
cases.case_name: World Bank Group v. Oxford University Press East Africa Limited and Oxford University Press Tanzania Limited (East Africa education textbook bribery)
cases.source_document: World Bank Group press release 2012-07-03; World Bank Integrity Vice Presidency (INT) investigation findings; Negotiated Resolution Agreement between WB and OUP entities; UK SFO High Court civil recovery order under Proceeds of Crime Act 2002
cases.notes: Seventh WB INT case in the corpus, bringing WB INT to 7 cases (ahead of FR PNF at 6). Debarment announced 3 July 2012 by the World Bank Group via Negotiated Resolution Agreement. Two entities debarred for 3 years: Oxford University Press East Africa Limited (OUPEA, Nairobi-based, regional publisher serving Burundi, Malawi, Rwanda, Sudan, Uganda) and Oxford University Press Tanzania Limited (OUPT, covering Tanzania mainland and Zanzibar). OUP parent received conditional non-debarment. Conduct: improper payments to government officials in Kenya and Tanzania between 2007 and 2010 to win competitive tenders and publishing contracts for school textbooks under two World Bank-financed education projects in East Africa. SELF-REPORTING PATTERN: in May 2011, INT investigators approached OUP about potential misconduct in Africa. Following this, OUP conducted an internal investigation into its operations and reported its findings to INT. The voluntary disclosure substantially shaped the resolution — 3 years debarment for the operating subsidiaries with conditional non-debarment for the parent, restitution of USD 500,000 (relatively modest compared to the GBP 1.9M parallel SFO civil recovery), and the parent's voluntary GBP 2M contribution to sub-Saharan African education charities. Settlement amount: USD 500,000 entered as the WB-side restitution.
