PARALLEL UK SFO CIVIL RECOVERY ACTION: distinct from the WB INT proceeding, the UK Serious Fraud Office obtained a High Court civil recovery order under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 against Oxford Publishing Limited (OPL, the OUP subsidiary that received dividends and fees from OUPEA and OUPT) in the amount of GBP 1,895,435 (approximately USD 2.96M at the time). OUP additionally agreed to contribute GBP 2,000,000 voluntarily to not-for-profit organizations for teacher training and other educational purposes in sub-Saharan Africa, plus paid the costs of the SFO investigation. The SFO action was civil recovery (not criminal prosecution) on the basis that OPL had received revenue derived from unlawful conduct (bribery) by OUPEA and OUPT subsidiaries. SECOND CROSS-PROSECUTOR REFERRAL PATTERN IN CORPUS: WB INT investigation triggered the parallel SFO action — INT referred matters to UK authorities for follow-up enforcement on the parent-company side. The WB press release from July 2012 explicitly notes 'working with the Serious Fraud Office also demonstrates the scope of collective action in deterring corruption impacting the progress of development.' This is structurally analogous to the Macmillan 2010 pattern (WB INT debarment + parallel UK SFO GBP 11M civil settlement order on the parent). The two patterns together establish a corpus-level pattern of WB INT proceedings against operating subsidiaries triggering UK SFO enforcement against UK-based parent companies, particularly in the education/publishing sector. WITHIN-SECTOR COMPARISON: this is the SECOND education/publishing sector case in the WB INT corpus, alongside Macmillan 2010 (Sudan MDTF). Both cases involve UK-headquartered publishing companies, both involve bribery to win textbook supply contracts on WB-financed education projects in Africa, both involve parallel UK SFO enforcement against the parent. Comparison points: Macmillan was sanctioned 6 years (longer duration, conducted in worse-faith — Macmillan attempted to bribe WB employees directly); OUP sanctioned 3 years (shorter duration, voluntarily self-reported to INT after May 2011 INT approach). Macmillan's parallel SFO civil settlement was GBP 11.3M (much larger than OUP's GBP 1.9M civil recovery + GBP 2M voluntary contribution). The differential is structurally meaningful: self-reporting and cooperation produced approximately 2x reduction in debarment duration and approximately 5x reduction in parallel UK monetary penalty. This within-sector calibration data point is directly relevant to future Phase C derivation work on cooperation-credit thresholds. Sanctions LIFTED in July 2015 after 3-year debarment expired. Dimensional category: administrative_sanctionable + bribery_channel + education_publishing_sector + self_reported + cross_debarment_eligible + int_referral_to_national_prosecutor + east_africa_education_projects + parallel_civil_recovery_order + within_sector_comparison_to_macmillan + kenya_tanzania_textbook_tenders. NOT markup_based. NOT cartel. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. WB INT slice now contains 7 cases spanning 6 sectors (infrastructure bridges, infrastructure hydropower, electronics/transportation, education/publishing [now 2 cases — Macmillan + OUP], telecommunications, roads infrastructure), duration range from 18 months to permanent, and three conduct topologies (individual bribery, individual fraud, multi-entity cartel). The within-sector comparison between Macmillan and OUP is the FIRST WITHIN-SECTOR DIVERSITY in the corpus and provides cooperation-credit calibration data unavailable when only one case per sector existed.

cases.legal_basis: Convention judiciaire d'intérêt public (CJIP) under Article 41-1-2 Code de procédure pénale (Loi Sapin II); signed 10 February 2025 between Procureur de la République financier and Paprec Group; validated 11 February 2025 by President of the Tribunal judiciaire de Paris (Stéphane Noël); EUR 17,538,990 amende d'intérêt public (with EUR 4.8M previously seized by Trésor public during information judiciaire, leaving EUR 12.7M outstanding); compliance program under AFA supervision; charges: blanchiment aggravé (aggravated money laundering of tax fraud), corruption active et passive de personnes chargées de mission de service public (active and passive bribery of persons entrusted with public service mission, Code pénal Art. 433-1 / 432-11), favoritisme et recel de favoritisme (favoritism and concealment of favoritism, Code pénal Art. 432-14), ententes illicites (unlawful agreement / cartel, Code de commerce L. 420-1 + Code pénal Art. 313-1)
cases.case_name: PNF v. Paprec Group (waste management cartel, favoritism, corruption, and money laundering CJIP)
cases.source_document: CJIP Paprec Group validated 11 February 2025 by TJ Paris; PNF press communications; Navacelle legal analysis; Nomos legal analysis; Brigade interdépartementale d'enquêtes de concurrence (BIEC) Article 40 CPP referral
cases.notes: Seventh FR PNF case in the corpus, bringing FR PNF to 7 cases (parity with WB INT). CJIP signed 10 February 2025 between Paprec Group (French waste management leader, third-largest French operator in energy recovery from waste) and the Procureur de la République financier Jean-François Bohnert; validated 11 February 2025 by President of the Tribunal judiciaire de Paris Stéphane Noël. AMOUNT: EUR 17,538,990 amende d'intérêt public, of which EUR 4.8M had already been seized by the Trésor public during the underlying information judiciaire (leaving EUR 12.7M outstanding to pay). At a EUR-USD exchange rate near parity for early 2025, this represents approximately USD 19M (entered as 19000000 in settlement_amount_usd). MULTI-CHARGE STRUCTURE — FIRST IN CORPUS for FR PNF: Paprec accepted the CJIP without admission of guilt on any charge, but the underlying procedural information judiciaire identified four distinct categories of suspected misconduct: (1) blanchiment aggravé — aggravated money laundering of tax fraud, evidenced by EUR 1.78M undeclared cash withdrawal; (2) corruption active et passive de personnes chargées de mission de service public — active bribery to obtain inside information about public tender opportunities, plus passive bribery counterpart on the receiving side (Code pénal Art. 433-1 active + Art. 432-11 passive); (3) favoritisme et recel de favoritisme — providing advantages in exchange for award of two specific public contracts (Code pénal Art. 432-14); (4) ententes illicites — horizontal cartel agreement with competitors on public procurement markets across multiple French departments (Toulouse, Grenoble, Isère, Mayenne, Haute-Garonne). This four-charge composite is the FIRST corpus entry that combines cartel + procurement favoritism + corruption + money laundering in a single proceeding. CONDUCT PERIOD: 2013 to 2022 (approximately 9 years of sustained misconduct). FIRST PNF CONCURRENCE/COMPETITION-LAW CJIP: this case is institutionally significant as the first time PNF used its powers in matters of competition-law infractions connected to penal infractions (per the Navacelle and Nomos legal analysis sources). The case was referred to PNF by the Brigade interdépartementale d'enquêtes de concurrence (BIEC) under Article 40 du CPP — illustrating the increasing 're-criminalization' of competition law in France and the growing PNF-Autorité de la concurrence coordination pattern. CROSS-JURISDICTIONAL STRUCTURAL PARALLEL: this Paprec case is the FR PNF analogue to the WB INT CRBC/NRIMP1 2009 cartel case (case 30). Both cases involve horizontal cartel coordination among bidders + procurement-side capture (favoritism in Paprec, bid-rigging in CRBC) producing structurally similar conduct topologies under different prosecutorial regimes. The Paprec case therefore extends the corpus's collusive_bid_rigging dimensional coverage from the WB INT slice into the FR PNF slice for the first time — directly relevant to future Phase C derivation work on the collusive_bid_rigging / procurement_favoritism dimensions which now have multi-jurisdictional anchor data. INDIVIDUAL CRIMINAL EXPOSURE PRESERVED: while the CJIP extinguishes the public action against Paprec as legal person, several physical persons remain mises en examen in the underlying information judiciaire, including former Paprec CEO Jean-Luc Petithuguenin (mis en examen May 2022), Philippe Marini (former LR president of the Senate finance commission and mayor of Compiègne), and Xavier Dugoin (former senator of Essonne). The two political figures are suspected of having actively participated in the bribery for obtaining public contracts. SECTOR NEW TO FR PNF SLICE: waste management. Prior FR PNF corpus sectors covered aerospace/defense (Airbus 2020, Airbus 2022), oil services (Bolloré 2021 ports), banking (Société Générale 2018), engineering consulting (Egis Avia 2019), construction/healthcare (Bouygues/Linkcity 2023). Waste management joins as the seventh sector covered in FR PNF. Dimensional category: bribery_channel + collusive_bid_rigging + procurement_favoritism + multi_charge_cjip + money_laundering_tax_fraud + waste_management_sector + french_domestic_procurement + multi_department_cartel + competition_law_criminal_repenalization + first_pnf_concurrence_cjip + cross_jurisdictional_parallel_to_wb_int_cartel_pattern. NOT markup_based in the classical sense (no documented markup ratio in the public CJIP communications). Not bribery_channel in the classical offshore-shell sense either — the bribery here was domestic French public officials, not foreign officials, more analogous to corruption_domestic_official patterns. MJPIS markup_floor_ratio remains 0.501. FR PNF slice now contains 7 cases at parity with WB INT, spanning 7 sectors and now including the cartel/favoritism conduct topology that previously appeared only in the WB INT slice. The cross-jurisdictional structural parallel between Paprec (FR PNF 2025) and CRBC (WB INT 2009) is corpus-level evidence that horizontal cartel + procurement capture is a structurally consistent conduct pattern across mature prosecution jurisdictions, providing direct calibration data for any future TCA rule targeting this topology.
